original_abstract:
Large "instruction-tuned" language models—those fine-tuned to respond to natural language commands—have shown a remarkable ability to generalize to new tasks in a zero-shot setting. However, the performance and breadth of these models are heavily constrained by the quality and quantity of human-written instruction data, which is often limited in scale, diversity, and creativity. To address this bottleneck, we introduce SELF-INSTRUCT, a framework that aligns pretrained language models with natural language instructions by bootstrapping from their own generations. 

Our pipeline works by generating new instructions, alongside corresponding input and output samples, directly from a language model. It then filters out low-quality or redundant entries before using the refined synthetic dataset to fine-tune the model itself. When applied to a vanilla GPT-3 model, SELF-INSTRUCT yields a 33% absolute improvement on the SUPER-NATURALINSTRUCTIONS benchmark, achieving performance nearly equal to InstructGPT-001—a model trained on proprietary user data and extensive human annotations. 

To further validate our approach, we curated a set of expert-written instructions for novel, user-oriented tasks. Human evaluation demonstrates that tuning GPT-3 with SELF-INSTRUCT significantly outperforms models trained on existing public instruction datasets and closes the performance gap with InstructGPT-001 to within 5%. SELF-INSTRUCT provides a virtually annotation-free method for aligning language models with human intent. We are releasing our dataset of 52K synthetic instructions and our evaluation suite to facilitate future research in instruction tuning.
humanized_abstract:
Large instruction-tuned language models are typically fine-tuned on large datasets of human-written instruction text in order to learn how to act on diverse commands. While these models can perform new tasks in a zero-shot fashion, their performance and applicability are strictly bounded by the size, diversity, and creativity of available instruction text. To break this bottleneck, we propose SELF-INSTRUCT (SELF-supervised language instructions by modeling the instructions the model itself can generate), a method for training a model to follow the natural language instructions it has learned to understand, by leveraging only the models own capacity for text generation.

As opposed to prior approaches that rely on an initial model to generate initial training data for subsequent model iterations, this method directly trains the model by learning from the output of a language model. The method generates new instructional text, with input and output data points, and discards the data points that are insufficient, unsuitable, or redundant. The synthetic data points that remain are used to train and/or to do further training on the model. The proposed method, SELF-INSTRUCT, improves the performance of a vanilla version of GPT-3 by 33% absolute on the SUPER-NATURALINSTRUCTIONS benchmark while only seeing the model train and/or further train on synthetic data generated by the output of a language model similar to the base model. In contrast, the performance is close to that of InstructGPT-001 which was trained on the proprietary user data of Eleme, with the model being greatly enriched by abundant annotations from humans.

We are releasing a new dataset of expertly written instructions for a diverse set of new, user-oriented tasks to validate our approach. Our human evaluation results show that GPT-3 model fine-tuned with SELF-INSTRUCT significantly outperforms all other models (including models fine-tuned with popular publicly available instruction datasets) by a large margin and is only a few points below InstructGPT-001. SELF-INSTRUCT is an almost fully annotation-free method for aligning large language models to user intent. We are open sourcing our 52K synthetic instructions dataset and accompanying benchmarks and invite the wider research community to build upon our work.

Large instruction-tuned language models are typically fine-tuned on large datasets of human-written instruction text in order to learn how to act on diverse commands. While these models can perform new tasks in a zero-shot fashion, their performance and applicability are strictly bounded by the size, diversity, and creativity of available instruction text.
